relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Boaz enters the story as a relative with land, standing, and power.
Ruth asks to work the gleaning ground, seeking favor without surrendering initiative.
Her chance brings her into Boaz's portion of the field.
Blessing is exchanged between landholder and laborers.
Boaz notices a worker not yet known within his household network.
The overseer identifies her by migration, ethnicity, and loyalty to Naomi.
Ruth has asked permission and worked through the morning with little rest.
Boaz offers continuity of work beside the young women.
Protection becomes concrete: no touching, access to water, and a field where she may work.
Ruth asks why a foreign woman has been recognized rather than ignored.
Boaz answers with the migration story and care already carried in her body.
He blesses the labor of one who has sought refuge under YHWH's wings.
Ruth receives comfort while remembering that she holds less status than the household workers.
At the meal she is seated, fed, satisfied, and given enough to carry home.
Her access expands, and humiliation is forbidden.
Workers are told to leave abundance within her reach without rebuke.
She labors until evening and beats out a substantial measure of barley.
The harvest and the meal's remainder cross the threshold into Naomi's hands.
Naomi asks where recognition entered Ruth's workday and blesses its source.
Covenant-kindness becomes visible again through a near kinsman with responsibility.
Ruth reports an invitation to remain through the whole harvest.
Naomi names safety plainly: stay where women work together and assault is restrained.
Ruth remains through barley and wheat, working the field while dwelling with Naomi.

contemplative_note: Ruth 2 refuses a spirituality that blesses poverty without changing working conditions. Divine wings appear through enforceable safety, water, food, rest, respect, and access to harvest. Ruth is neither a charity object nor a romantic prize; she is a migrant worker whose labor, initiative, and bodily safety matter.
